speaker: interviewer
text: We're conducting an interview with Ruth Foster in Winchmore Hill, London, England, and this is tape number five. You were just about to get to your--

speaker: interviewee
text: On the 20-- it must have been the 20th of-- or 21st of-- August. I arrived by train in my hometown because there were-- was very little bomb damage so the last track of the railways were still going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your friend still with you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my friend-- my friend went back to Dresden from Berlin. We parted, and I swam through the river into the American zone. And then I came into the British zone back to Lingen. I went back to these friends of my parents. They had bakers and confectioners, and they took me in.

speaker: interviewer
text: They weren't Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There were no Jews. I'm the only-- in Germany, you have to register if you stay longer than 48 hours with the police. So the second-- the next day I had to go to the police. They gave me clothes, my friends as well. I had to register with the police, and I was the only one of 100 so that had survived from Lingen, had come out of the concentration camp. When I came to the police station, this police-- the policeman who took my father to the train to Buchenwald and us to Osnabruck to the-- where-- from where we were sent via Bielefeld to Riga, he was sitting in his office. And when he saw me, the man fainted. And he started to cry. It's quite unbelievable when he saw me. And when he asked me about my parents and about all the others and so-- so I registered. And so he said, you know, very strange thing is I had to vacate my house. And in my house are two officers billeted, and they both speak perfect German. I must say, my hometown and the whole area was occupied by the Polish forces under British command. That was the occupation army, the British forces, but these were the Polish forces under British command. They came from Normandy with the invasion to France, Belgium, Holland, and into Germany. My husband also took part. He has quite a story. It took part with the invasion of Germany, of Normandy, at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said one is a doctor, and the other one is a quartermaster. And they both speak perfect German. He said, I will tell them that you are the only one has returned to Lingen. The next day, he came to these people with whom I stayed. And he said, they very much want to talk to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time it was-- there was no fraternizing. They were not-- the soldiers were not allowed to talk-- go to German houses. They'd called non-fraternizing. They weren't allowed to have any contact with the civilian people, with the Germans. So I had to go to them where they were billeted.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went there. And as it turned out, after I was 48 hours in Lingen, in my hometown, I met my husband. A year later we were married, and I came to England. What was he doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a doctor with this regiment. He was a doctor, yes, with the regiment, Polish Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your parents' home?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my parents' home, people were living there. And I got the house back, which I sold when I came to England. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anything left of your parents' belongings?

speaker: interviewee
text: From my parents I only got with friend-- they brought some table linen, some bed linen, and-- and few little bits to neighbors, which they kept. But just these neighbors were bombed, and they lost part of the stuff of us-- of ours in an air raid. But yes, so as I say that every story, however cruel and sad it is, has a happy ending, at least mine had. And I've been asked many times if I can ever forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I tell them-- I tell them in Germany when I speak to schools. I tell them in England here. It is like when you stand on a lake, and you drop a stone into a lake, you first have small ripples. The ripples get larger and larger, and then they disappear, and the surface is calm. But the stone is still at the bottom. And that is a stone. It's still there [INAUDIBLE]. What date did you actually come to England?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came on the 17th of August, 1947. I had great difficulties to come to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you already married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was married on the-- on the 28th of November, 1946, in Germany. Our chuppah was at my-- at my-- my relative, my cousin's, who survived [INAUDIBLE] in Bremen. And the civil marriage was in [INAUDIBLE], a town in Germany where my husband was stationed, his regiment. My husband went back with the regiment in February of 1947 to England, and so did all the wives because lots of the soldiers had in the meantime married Polish girls, because it was a Polish army. There were lots of DPs, you know, displaced persons they had married. And they all went to England. And when I was supposed to go to England, I was not allowed entry into England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was born in Germany. Even though by that time I was a Polish subject, I had married a Polish subject. I was not allowed to enter England, so I was left behind. And they took from-- the went in March '47, and this took until August '47 until my husband went to the chief rabbi of the forces, a Dr. [? Brody. ?] And he intervened with [? Barnet ?] Jenner who was an MP. He is a father of [INAUDIBLE] Jenner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a law in Parliament passed that those who have soldiers, who have married German citizens, were allowed to come to England. So I was the first one born in Germany that came into England. That was worse than the camp sitting in Germany, and my husband was over here, especially when you haven't got anybody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. When-- when was your daughter born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter was born in October '48.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you now have two grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two grandchildren and a son-in-a-law. And-- yes. And from the large family, my mother, there were five brothers and five sisters. They were 10. My father were five brothers and two sisters. They were seven. They all perished with their spouses and their children. From mother's side, we were-- we were 30 cousins, and six have survived-- survived. Five. Five or six survived and from father three-- father's side three. There were about 18 cousins. And one of them had died-- has died since, one of the three survivors from father's side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about this photograph, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it's my grandmother--

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: --from mother's side. Her name was Frauke Grunberg. And her maiden name was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how long ago this picture was taken or when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember her like this, so it must have been taken in the 1930s, early 1930s, because she died in '35 or so. She was a very pious woman, very religious. You see her there sitting. That's her prayer book, which she always had, and her teapot which was kept hot on a little stove. In Dutch, they call it [DUTCH] And the [DUTCH] would have hot coals in it, the teapot on top. She was always sitting in the summer outside. They had like a trellis, you know, like a [NON-ENGLISH] [LAUGHS] front of the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Would you like to tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are the 10 children of my grandmother Frauke, the brothers and sisters of my late mother. And the first one is Tante Rosa, Aunt Rosa. You want any particulars about her?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. You can just--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then Uncle Hermon and [? Rachel, ?] Uncle [? Ern, ?] Aunt Mary, Uncle Phillip, my mother Caroline, Uncle Max, Uncle-- Auntie Flora, and Uncle Willie.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they all perished?

speaker: interviewee
text: They all perished with their spouses and children. Like I said, none of my grandmother's children have a grave, none of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, that's my father, Wilhelm Heilbronn and my mother, Caroline Heilbronn. And underneath, this last picture, shortly before we were deported, that's my parents and me in front of one of the Juden houses in Lingen. And right in the corner there's a picture from the First World War, and my father was standing there. He's the second from the right as a German soldier.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me. I must have been three or four years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was taken in your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in my hometown. And my mother always had that hanging in the window of the photographer for years. [LAUGHS] That's how good that was. Not for years. It's exaggerate, but a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of the train which took us from Osnabruck, where we were collected, to Bielefeld. And this train stops at the station or the good station in Bielefeld. And you see here the people coming onto the-- onto the train, joining us, then eventually a thousand people, and we are taken to Riga. You see them always there as much as they could carry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. That was a German photographer took it for a newspaper.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And after the war, cousin of mine who lives in Germany, the spouse of one of the one I said who had died since, he lives in Germany. And he got it from the-- I think it was from the The newspaper or the magazine, had it and he got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Could you tell us about this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is the entrance to the ghetto in Riga. And it says a warning to the people who come too near it that they would be shot, or if they tried to get out of it, or others get into-- into it. That's-- that's just a warning in German and in Latvian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is a view of a street in the Riga ghetto. Here you see the barbed wire and the ramshackle houses on the right. On the opposite side is already the free town of Riga, but on the right of the picture here you see those little houses, the barbed wires. And you can't see here the control towers, but that's on the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, This is-- this picture, you see how the Jews were taken from the ghetto every morning to their place of work and accompanied by a German, either SS or soldier. These are [GERMAN]-- these are women. They're going to work from the ghetto into town. I don't know where they were working. You saw these columns, hundreds of columns in the morning, you know, of Jews going to different places to work. And that's my-- a picture of my husband in uniform. He was a doctor and a captain in the Polish Army under British command. He was born in Poland in Brody near Lviv near Lemberg and studied in Vienna. Fled to France, joined the Polish Army, came to Scotland, and with invasion to Normandy, he came to France, Belgium, Holland, and then into Germany where he met me and we got married. He died in 1979.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what year was that picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This must have been taken in 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about this picture, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was taken on our wedding day, on the 28th of November, 1946 in Bremen in the house of my cousins who survived Theresienstadt where the chuppah took place. And the rabbi who officiated was a close a squadron leader doctor, [INAUDIBLE]. He came from Liverpool.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my daughter Caroline named after my mother Caroline. And she actually has two names, Francis Caroline, but we call her Caroline Francis after my husband's mother, Franny. And her husband Henry-- Henry. They were married on our silver wedding day, on the 28th of November 1971.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken just recently at a function they went to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us who's in this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's my granddaughter Mandy, Mandy Blank. She was born on the 1st of March, 1974. We are all very proud of her. She is-- is at Cambridge, King's College, where she did science and did very well indeed. And now studies for her PhD in the perception of psychology. And good luck to you, Mandy. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my grandson Martin, Martin [? Joseph ?] Blank. He was born on the 14th of August, 1978. And-- and he is studying now for his A levels. He's doing very well at school. We are rather proud of him as well. And good luck to him, whatever you do, Martin, and remember your old granny. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Listen to that wind. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's me with my only surviving cousins from mother's side. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] My cousin [? Fridel ?] on the left, she survived Auschwitz. That-- comes me and then comes my cousin [? Marianna. ?] She survived [INAUDIBLE]. My cousin Al, who survived Auschwitz, and my cousin [? Arnold ?] who was in Switzerland during the war. And that's the only one, really, who escaped the concentration camp. The six survivors are the only ones of about 28 cousins from mother's side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Can you tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is-- is a picture taken at-- oh, the commemoration of the memorial stone, which was erected in the hometown or the home village where my father lived. His family lived there, the Heilbronns, in 1680. And only Heilbronns lived there, and my-- and the village has put a stone up in the memory of those who have perished and the names now on that as well. In this picture, I'm on the left, my cousin Mike and his sister Berta, who were also survivors, have died since. And my cousin Anna and [? Hella. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So the three of us are the only survivors. We were five from a large family. The rest all perished from father's side. All cousins and uncles and aunts, they all perished. But now those two have died as well. There are only three left. Would you like to tell us about this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a memorial stone which the town Lingen erected in commemoration of the Jewish families who were killed by the Germans. And the words on-- at the top is taken from the laments. It means in English, 'for those I cry.' And underneath are all the names of the families who perished. And I'm the only survivor of the lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just say something?

speaker: interviewer
text: Just tell us about the [INAUDIBLE] you have.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is my original zebra dress as we used to call it, zebra stripes. Prison garb, which was handed to me in Stutthof after we came out of the showers, which were originally gas chambers, where in my text I said, we waited for the water which never came. And later on were told it was gas chambers. So at the other end, we were handed these. And I was in this-- this dress until the end of the war, until March '45, and Russian deloused it. Was full of lice. And gave it back to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell us about this jumper please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is a jumper which was given to us in the labor camp where we did woodwork, when we came from Stutthof. The commandant went to Stutthof and get some jumpers for us, some sweaters, which we actually wore as leggings. The arms we put our legs in to keep our bodies warm. And as a top we had paper and everything under our dresses. And the Star of David I acquired later on, after the war, and I just put it on there. But this what we had to wear on our outer clothing.
